sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 61, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ACTION7 is NOT undo. When trail fully extended, ACTION7 moves player+targets up by 6 rows. When trail not fully extended, ACTION7 extends trail right. Now at rows 24-29 (player), 25-28 (targets).
- Goal model: Still unclear. Panel shows red, green, blue order. Trail has green, blue, red.
- Action model: UP/DOWN moves player vertically, RIGHT extends trail. Need to figure out how to "collect" targets.
- Plan: Try ACTION7 one more time to continue moving up - maybe reaching a specific row completes the level.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 60 of about 61 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
ACTION7 moved everything up by 6 rows again:
- Player: rows 18-23 (was 24-29)
- Targets: rows 19-22 (was 25-28)
- Trail: cols 16-46 (unchanged)
- State node: 44 (new state)
Level not completed. I'm at step 61 of 61. I have very few actions left.
Let me think about what to do. The player is now at rows 18-23, which is near the top of the board. The targets are at rows 19-22.
Looking at the vertical bar on the left side, it has different colored segments. Maybe this is a clue about the desired position.
Actually, I notice that the player is now at rows 18-23, which is the same row range as the initial state (when the game started). Let me check if this is the initial state.
Looking at the history, the initial state had the player at rows 18-23 and targets at rows 19-22. So ACTION7 has returned the player and targets to the initial position!
But the trail is still extended (cols 16-46), and the targets are still on the trail. So this is not exactly the initial state.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to have the player at a specific position with the targets in a specific order.
Actually, I've been wasting too many actions. I'm at step 61 of 61, and I still haven't solved the level. I have very few actions left.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to use LEFT to retract the trail and then use ACTION7 to move up again. Or maybe I need to use DOWN to move the player down.
Actually, let me try ACTION7 one more time to see if it does something different now that the player is at the top of the board.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: ACTION7 moved player+targets up by 6 rows again. Now at rows 18-23 (player), 19-22 (targets) - near initial position. Trail still extended at cols 16-46. Level not completed.
Goal model: Still unclear. Need to find the correct configuration.
Plan: Try ACTION7 again - maybe at the top it does something different (wraps around or completes the level).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try ACTION7 one more time at top position
result = action(['ACTION7'])
print("Result:", last_action_result)
print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done'))
print("Score:", last_action_result.get('score'))
print("State node:", last_action_result.get('state_node_id'))

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Check all targets
for color, name in [('R', 'red'), ('b', 'blue'), ('N', 'green')]:
    targets = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == color and n['boundary'][0][0] < 50]
    for t in targets:
        rows = [p[0] for p in t['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in t['boundary']]
        print(f"{name}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check player
player = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18]
if player:
    p = player[0]
    rows = [r[0] for r in p['boundary']]
    cols = [r[1] for r in p['boundary']]
    print(f"Player: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
